main_sections.title: The Cherry Tree
main_sections.prose_summary: The Cherry Tree is a tender narrative by Ruskin Bond that traces the bond between a young boy, Rakesh, and a cherry tree he grows from a single seed over several years in the Himalayan foothills near Mussoorie. Rakesh, who lives with his grandfather while attending school, walks home one day eating cherries bought from the Mussoorie bazaar. On his grandfather's gentle prompting that luck must be put to use, he plants the last cherry seed in a soft, shaded corner of the garden. He then forgets about it entirely, absorbed in the rhythms of school and cricket. Spring brings the first surprise — a tiny rooted shoot that Rakesh mistakes for a twig before joyfully calling his grandfather. The two bend together in wonder over a four-inch sapling. Seasons accumulate around the growing tree: monsoon rains speed its growth to two feet, only for a wayward goat to strip all its leaves; a grass-cutter's scythe slices it in two; field-mice cluster in the cottage roof under winter snow while the tree bends low under its weight. Yet each time the cherry tree recovers, sending out new shoots with quiet determination, mirroring Rakesh's own steady growth from a six-year-old boy to a sturdy nine-year-old. One February morning, grandfather spots the first pale pink blossom and calls out in disbelief and delight. In subsequent years blossoms multiply; bees feed on nectar; bulbuls and scarlet minivets feast on small cherries; a praying-mantis and a hairy caterpillar arrive as uninvited guests. Eventually grandfather finds the perfect shade of the fully grown tree, reclining in a cane chair and watching leaves dance in the breeze, while Rakesh lies beneath it gazing up at the blue sky and distant mountains. In the dusk, with crickets and cicadas filling the forest, Rakesh asks why this tree is special among so many. His grandfather answers simply: 'We planted it ourselves.' Touching the smooth bark, Rakesh whispers, 'Is this what it feels to be God?' The story celebrates patience, the quiet triumph of nature, and the deep human satisfaction of nurturing life from a single seed.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Arrange the events in order of occurrence in the story. Two have been done for you. Share your answers with your classmates and the teacher.

question_bank.stem: Rakesh discovered that the small, well-rooted twig, had grown into a cherry tree.
Rakesh was sent to live with his grandfather in Mussoorie.
Rakesh pressed the cherry seed into the soft soil with his thumb.
The cherry tree grew quickly after the rains arrived early.
Rakesh bought a bunch of cherries and ate most of them.
A goat entered the garden and ate all the leaves of the young cherry tree.
Rakesh's parents grew maize, rice, and barley in a village. (1)
Rakesh sprinkled water on the cherry tree and surrounded it with pebbles.
Grandfather told Rakesh that cherry trees were tough.
Rakesh kept rolling the last cherry seed in his mouth.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Do you think the cherry tree would survive? Why do you think so?
Complete the table given below based on your understanding of Part II of the story. One example has been done for you. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: A woman cut the cherry tree while cutting grass. → (i) Grandfather scolded the woman.
question_bank.table:
Cause|Effect
A woman cut the cherry tree while cutting grass.|(i) Grandfather scolded the woman.
2.|(ii) Rakesh was thinner but stronger when he came back.
A hairy caterpillar started eating the leaves of the cherry tree.|(iii)
4.|(iv) Rakesh and Grandfather gazed at the tree as though it was a miracle.
Rakesh tasted one of the small cherries during summer.|(v)

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: "Have a cherry, Grandfather," he said, as soon as he saw his grandfather in the garden. Grandfather took one cherry and Rakesh promptly ate the other two. He kept the last seed in his mouth for some time, rolling it round and round on his tongue until all the tang had gone. Then he placed the seed on the palm of his hand and studied it. "Are cherry seeds lucky?" asked Rakesh. "Of course." "Then I'll keep it." "Nothing is lucky if you put it away. If you want luck, you must put it to some use."

question_bank.stem: Substitute the underlined phrase with a correct word from the extract. The Kho-kho captain changed her strategy quickly and without delay when a new player came in from the opposing team.

What does Rakesh's action of rolling the cherry seed in his mouth most likely suggest about his mood at that moment? A. He is feeling impatient and restless. B. He is enjoying the taste and the moment. C. He is bored and has nothing better to do. D. He is thoughtful and feeling playful.
options: He is feeling impatient and restless.
He is enjoying the taste and the moment.
He is bored and has nothing better to do.
He is thoughtful and feeling playful.

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence suitably with reference to Rakesh and his grandfather. Rakesh's decision to plant the seed shows that ______________ . (he respects and values his grandfather's advice / he has a close bond with his grandfather and a willingness to learn from him)
How does the grandfather's advice reflect his view about luck and effort?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extract and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: Winter came early. The cherry tree bent low with the weight of snow. Field-mice sought shelter in the roof of the cottage. The road from the valley was blocked, and for several days there was no newspaper, and this made Grandfather quite grumpy. His stories began to have unhappy endings. In February it was Rakesh's birthday. He was nine—and the tree was four, but almost as tall as Rakesh.

question_bank.stem: Complete the following sentence suitably. The phrase 'field-mice sought shelter in the roof of the cottage' suggests that _____________.

What can be inferred about the cherry tree's toughness from the line, 'The cherry tree bent low with the weight of snow'? A. The weight of the tree was more than the snow. B. The tree could withstand harsh weather. C. The tree was growing close to the ground. D. The snow gave support to the tree.
options: The weight of the tree was more than the snow.
The tree could withstand harsh weather.
The tree was growing close to the ground.
The snow gave support to the tree.

question_bank.stem: How did the early winter affect Grandfather's mood and storytelling?

Complete the sentence by choosing the correct option. The comparison of Rakesh's age to the cherry tree's age symbolises _______________. (the tree's growth paralleling Rakesh's own development / the tree standing as a dear one on Rakesh's birthday)
Why did Rakesh's parents send him to Mussoorie?
Why did Rakesh let the praying-mantis remain on the branch of the cherry tree?
How is life in the Himalayan foothills described in the story?
What might Rakesh mean when he says, "Is this what it feels to be God?" at the end of the story?
How does the writer describe the relationship between Rakesh and his grandfather?
What can you infer about the connection between humans and nature based on the story?
Imagine Rakesh reflects on his wonderful experience of watching a seed being transformed into a full-grown cherry tree. Write how he must have felt.

listening.transcript_text:
SON: Amma, could you help me with this project?
MOTHER: Why not? What's the project about?
SON: It's about the Chipko Movement. I have to make a PowerPoint presentation and talk about it in class.
MOTHER: That's good! Chipko Movement was all about hugging trees to prevent them from being cut, isn't it? Such an inspiring movement it was!
SON: Yes, it was the first time that common people came together for the noble cause of saving trees. It showed how people can also make a difference. Have you heard or read about something similar?
MOTHER: Yes, of course. The success of the Chipko Movement led to the Appiko Movement.
SON: Really? Doesn't appiko mean 'hugging' in Kannada? So, did they also hug trees to protect them?
MOTHER: Yes they did. In this movement people not only hugged trees to protect them but also regenerated the forest areas in the Western Ghats.
SON: That's great! This reminds me of what my teacher shared about Saalumarada Thimakka last week. She is a Padma Shri awardee.
MOTHER: What an amazing personality! I've heard about her and I'm interested to know what your teacher has shared with you.
SON: She is an Indian environmentalist who has been planting trees since 1948. She has planted over 8000 trees in the area around her village.
MOTHER: Wonderful!
SON: What I admire about her is the commitment she has towards conserving the environment.
MOTHER: Incredible! That is one superwoman! But do you know who the Forest Man of India is?
SON: Yes, I've read about him. It is Jadav Payeng, from Assam. He too is a Padma Shri awardee. Isn't he the one who single-handedly turned a barren land of around a thousand acres into a lush green forest? He did so by planting nearly forty million trees.
MOTHER: Yes, you're absolutely right. It was not something that happened overnight. It took him thirty years to achieve this. This forest has become a habitat for different kinds of animals including migratory birds.
SON: Fascinating, isn't it? I've read a book and watched a documentary in school about him which has really inspired me.
MOTHER: I'm glad that you have begun to care about the environment too.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a son and his mother. As you listen, select four true statements from 1–7 given below.

question_bank.stem: The mother encouraged her son to deliver a presentation on the Chipko Movement.
The son was surprised to know about the Appiko Movement.
The son agreed that the Chipko Movement was about people uniting to make an impact.
The mother expressed a desire to share more details about Saalumarada Thimakka.
The mother was curious to hear about Saalumarada Thimakka.
The mother and son were in disagreement about the work of Jadav Payeng.
The son found the information on Jadav Payeng wonderful.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Work in pairs. Read aloud the following words from the text. Write the number of syllables in each word. Check your answers with your classmates and teacher. Now, read the words aloud again with the beats. Check if the number of syllables matches with the number of beats. Words: mountains, grandfather, tender, disturbed, caterpillar, Himalayan, performed, intention, slope, newspaper, favourite.

Speak about Van Mahotsav Diwas and its significance. Before you speak in the class or assembly, prepare yourself on the following points: When is it celebrated? Who celebrates it? Why is it celebrated? How is it celebrated? What is the message it conveys?

Complete the article for a magazine on 'Trees–Our Lifeline'. Use the words and phrases given in the box below: absorb carbon dioxide, Moreover, our planet, reduces pollution, Additionally, would stop existing, ensures a better future, climate control, Trees–Our Lifeline, To conclude, If trees were not there. Fill in the blanks (i) to (x) to complete the article.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blank (i): Title of the article
Fill in blank (ii): Without them, life as we know ________________.
Fill in blank (iii): Trees ________________ and release oxygen, helping us breathe.
Fill in blank (iv): This process also ________________ and keeps the environment clean.
Fill in blank (v): ________________, trees give us fruits, nuts, and other foods.
Fill in blank (vi): Trees also help in ________________ and prevention of soil erosion.
Fill in blank (vii): ________________, the air would be polluted, and we would struggle to breathe.
Fill in blank (viii): ________________ would be barren, lacking food, shelter, and life-giving greenery.
Fill in blank (ix): ________________, trees are true friends.
Fill in blank (x): Protecting and planting trees ________________ for everyone on Earth.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Each word list has one word that does not match with the other words. Identify the odd word. One example has been done for you.

question_bank.stem: chop, trim, slice, mince — Example: ('chop', 'slice' and 'mince' are words used for cutting with a knife; whereas 'trim' is to cut a part with a pair of scissors)
plodded, galloped, scrambled, trudged
tender, delicate, soft, smooth
stared, peered, pecked, gazed
growing, blossoming, progressing, planting

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose the most suitable taste words given in the box below and write against the food items that follow. Words: sweet, sour, tangy, bitter, spicy, salty, bland (tasteless). Food items: tamarind, jaggery, neem leaves, paneer (cottage cheese), lemon, pickle, a dish of vegetables. Now, use any five taste words and make sentences of your own.

question_bank.stem: tamarind
jaggery
neem leaves
paneer (cottage cheese)
lemon
pickle
a dish of vegetables

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Choose the correct antonyms for the textual words given below from the box. There are two extra words that you will not need. Box: growing, ordinary, unprotected, cheerful, hard, wide, fasting, shaky, fussing, coldness. Words: tender, sturdy, narrow, warmth, feasting, grumpy, sheltered, special.

question_bank.stem: tender
sturdy
narrow
warmth
feasting
grumpy
sheltered
special

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read the following lines from the text and underline all the verbs and identify the form of tense. 1. Rakesh was on his way home from school when he bought the cherries. 2. Rakesh was looking for Grandfather without finding him in any of his favourite places around the house. 3. Rakesh and Grandfather gazed at the tree as though it had performed a miracle. The forms of tenses are Simple Past Tense (1 and 3), Past Progressive Tense (2), and Past Perfect Tense (3).

question_bank.stem: Identify the tense forms in the given sentences.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Now, fill in the blanks with the correct form of Simple Past, Past Progressive, or Past Perfect Tenses of the verbs given in brackets. (i) I was sitting (sit) with my grandparents in the garden… As we (ii) ______________ (talk), I (iii) ______________ (realise) how much wisdom they (iv) ______________ (impart) to me over the years. While I (v) ______________ (listen) to their stories for hours together, I (vi) ______________ (remember) the times we (vii) ______________ (spend) together planting flowers and learning about life. By the time I left, the sun (viii) ______________ (set), and I (ix) ______________ (feel) a deep sense of gratitude for the bond we (x) ______________ (nurture) through love, care, and countless memories.

question_bank.stem: Fill in blank (ii): As we ______________ (talk)
Fill in blank (iii): I ______________ (realise)
Fill in blank (iv): they ______________ (impart)
Fill in blank (v): While I ______________ (listen)
Fill in blank (vi): I ______________ (remember)
Fill in blank (vii): the times we ______________ (spend)
Fill in blank (viii): the sun ______________ (set)
Fill in blank (ix): I ______________ (feel)
Fill in blank (x): the bond we ______________ (nurture)

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Study the punctuation marks in the following sentences from the text. Now, punctuate the following paragraph appropriately: 'every sunday roshni would visit her grandparents house on their farm come sit grandpa would say patting the bench on their porch roshni loved these moments where the world slowed down and stories flowed like magic grandma would bring tea her eyes twinkling with joy im excited to hear about your younger days the adventures the lessons and the memories roshni said eagerly grandpa smiled softly his wrinkled hands resting on the cane ah the old days… when everything was simple and happiness was all we had roshni could feel the warmth of those memories he sat in silence bound not by words but by a timeless bond'

question_bank.stem: Punctuate the given paragraph appropriately using the rules learned.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Did you know that the banyan tree is the National Tree of India? Our country has age-old banyan trees. Some of them are: 1. The Great Banyan, located in Shibpur, Howrah, near Kolkata. 2. Dodda Aalada Mara, located in Kethohalli village, near Bengaluru. 3. Thimmamma Marrimanu, in Anantapur District in Andhra Pradesh. It was recorded as the largest tree specimen in the world in the Guinness Book of World Records in 1989.

Kailash Sankhala, 'The Tiger Man of India', was an Indian biologist and conservationist. He carried out a census of the tigers in India in 1971. Later, because of his studies, he was appointed the first director of Project Tiger—an initiative to prevent the extinction of the Indian Tiger. He was awarded the Padma Shri in 1992. Now, find more information about such Indian environmentalists and share with your classmates.

question_bank.stem: Find more information about Indian environmentalists and share with your classmates.
